title: The Clash of Civilizations and the Remaking of World Order
description: Argues that in the post-Cold War world, conflict will be driven primarily by cultural and civilizational differences rather than ideological or economic factors.
key_concepts: Eight major civilizations defining global politics
excerpt: Argues that post-Cold War global conflict will be driven not by ideology or economics but by cultural and civilizational differences between the world's major cultural blocs.
keyIdeas: The primary axis of global conflict shifted from ideological to cultural after the Cold War
Eight major civilizations define the new world order, with the West facing rising challengers
Modernization does not equal Westernization — non-Western societies can modernize while preserving distinct identities
Fault line conflicts between civilizations, especially Islam-West and China-US, will define geopolitics
keyTakeaways: Cultural identity, not political ideology, is the primary source of post-Cold War conflict
Non-Western societies can modernize economically while reinforcing, not abandoning, their cultural identities
The West's belief in universal values is dangerous and provokes civilizational backlash
Core states anchor civilizations and rally kin-countries during fault line conflicts
whoShouldRead: Students of international relations and geopolitics
Readers seeking to understand post-Cold War geopolitical frameworks
Those studying the relationship between culture, religion, and global conflict
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking rigorous empirical or data-driven analysis
Those uncomfortable with broad civilizational generalizations
Readers looking for detailed intra-civilizational analysis
